reasoning: The finding is a false positive as described. The key facts: (1) The option fbrfg_favicon_non_interactive_api_request is written only from admin/class-favicon-by-realfavicongenerator-admin.php:274, inside install_new_favicon() which verifies a WP nonce at line 233 AND validates a second nonce embedded in the API response custom_parameter at line 248. (2) The value stored is getNonInteractiveAPIRequest(), which comes from parsing a JSON response fetched from the hardcoded URL https://realfavicongenerator.net (not user-supplied). (3) The automatic update cron path (common.php:473) calls set_favicon_configured() without passing the $non_interactive_api_request parameter, so it cannot update that option. (4) There is no direct low-privilege write path to this option. (5) The 'SSRF' label is a misnomer: the outbound request destination at run_non_interactive_api_request() is always the hardcoded URL https://realfavicongenerator.net/api/favicon (line 486) - an attacker cannot control the target URL, only potentially the POST body content. (6) For an attacker to exploit this they would need to either: compromise the remote realfavicongenerator.net API to return a malicious non_interactive_request value, OR gain write access to the WP database directly (in which case SSRF is not the most interesting attack). Neither constitutes a practical attack path against a normal installation. The 'second-order SSRF' characterization is inaccurate - this is at most an API parameter pollution risk requiring remote API compromise, not an attacker-reachable vulnerability.
